title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: vignette shows basic workflow msPCA built-mtcars dataset. compute sparse principal components, inspect solution print() summary() S3 methods, compare sparse result dense PCA.

title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: Install package directly CRAN.
code: install.packages("msPCA") library(msPCA)

title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: work correlation matrix mtcars ask two 4-sparse principal components default orthogonality constraint. print() shows sparse loading matrix restricted union active variables, together percentage variance explained number non-zero loadings per component. summary() gives fuller breakdown: per-PC table variance explained sparsity, followed pairwise feasibility violation matrix can see glance well orthogonality constraint satisfied pair components.
code: Sigma <- cor(mtcars)  set.seed(42) res <- mspca(Sigma, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), verbose = FALSE) print(res, Sigma) #>  #> msPCA solution: 2 sparse PCs #> Pct. variance explained: 32.45835 27.98031  #> Non-zero loadings per PC: 4 4  #>  #> Sparse PCs #>            [,1]       [,2] #> mpg   0.4994877  0.0000000 #> cyl  -0.4952709  0.0000000 #> disp -0.5096594  0.0000000 #> hp    0.0000000 -0.5180512 #> wt   -0.4954454  0.0000000 #> qsec  0.0000000  0.5056635 #> vs    0.0000000  0.4935970 #> carb  0.0000000 -0.4819641 summary(res, Sigma) #>  #> msPCA summary: 2 sparse PC(s) #> Input type   : Sigma  #> Runtime (s)  : 0.01  #>  #> Per-component statistics: #>   PC nonzero variance       fve cumulative_fve #>  PC1       4 3.570419 0.3245835      0.3245835 #>  PC2       4 3.077834 0.2798031      0.6043866 #>  #> Pairwise feasibility violations (upper triangle): #>     PC1 PC2 #> PC1   .   0 #> PC2   .   . #> Total: 0e+00

what: Working from the raw data matrix
title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: default (type = "Sigma") first argument covariance/correlation matrix. Set type = "X" pass raw data matrix instead (rows observations, columns variables). type = "X", msPCA applies algorithm data directly: matrix–vector product Σβ=X⊤(Xβ)/(n−1)\Sigma \beta = X^\top (X \beta) / (n - 1) computed without ever forming p×pp \times p matrix. mathematically equivalent scalable p≫np \gg n. preprocessing arguments control matrix implicitly used: center (default TRUE) subtracts column means. scale (default FALSE) divides column standard deviations; set TRUE operate correlation matrix. divisor selects normalization: "n-1" (default, matching cov/cor) "n". scale = TRUE divisor = "n-1", raw-data call reproduces correlation-matrix result . type = "X", variance figures stored inside object, C need passed print() summary(). dual interface available single-component tpm().
code: X <- as.matrix(mtcars)  set.seed(42) res_X <- mspca(X, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), type = "X", scale = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) print(res_X) #>  #> msPCA solution: 2 sparse PCs #> Pct. variance explained: 32.45835 27.98031  #> Non-zero loadings per PC: 4 4  #>  #> Sparse PCs #>            [,1]       [,2] #> mpg   0.4994875  0.0000000 #> cyl  -0.4952714  0.0000000 #> disp -0.5096593  0.0000000 #> hp    0.0000000 -0.5180511 #> wt   -0.4954451  0.0000000 #> qsec  0.0000000  0.5056627 #> vs    0.0000000  0.4935983 #> carb  0.0000000 -0.4819636

title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: Sparse PCA requires constraint prevent redundancy components. default (feasibilityConstraintType = 0) enforces orthogonality loading vectors. Setting feasibilityConstraintType = 1 instead enforces zero pairwise correlation resulting scores. choice can lead different solutions variables strongly correlated.
code: set.seed(42) res_corr <- mspca(Sigma, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4),                   feasibilityConstraintType = 1, verbose = FALSE) print(res_corr, Sigma) #>  #> msPCA solution: 2 sparse PCs #> Pct. variance explained: 24.72306 22.81602  #> Non-zero loadings per PC: 4 4  #>  #> Sparse PCs #>            [,1]        [,2] #> hp   -0.3117412  0.00000000 #> drat  0.0000000  0.33662426 #> wt    0.0000000 -0.08659835 #> qsec  0.6737896  0.00000000 #> vs    0.2785136  0.00000000 #> am    0.0000000  0.62421437 #> gear  0.0000000  0.69967225 #> carb -0.6093071  0.00000000 summary(res_corr, Sigma, feasibilityConstraintType = 1) #>  #> msPCA summary: 2 sparse PC(s) #> Input type   : Sigma  #> Runtime (s)  : 0.244  #>  #> Per-component statistics: #>   PC nonzero variance       fve cumulative_fve #>  PC1       4 2.719537 0.2472306      0.2472306 #>  PC2       4 2.509762 0.2281602      0.4753908 #>  #> Pairwise feasibility violations (upper triangle): #>     PC1          PC2 #> PC1   . 9.620782e-05 #> PC2   .            . #> Total: 9.620782e-05

title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: utility functions feasibility_violation_off() fraction_variance_explained() can called directly custom reporting comparing solutions across methods.
code: # Orthogonality and zero-correlation violations for the default solution feasibility_violation_off(Sigma, res$x_best, feasibilityConstraintType = 0) #> [1] 0 feasibility_violation_off(Sigma, res$x_best, feasibilityConstraintType = 1) #> [1] 2.335601  # Total and per-PC fraction of variance explained fraction_variance_explained(Sigma, res$x_best) #> [1] 0.6043866 fraction_variance_explained_perPC(Sigma, res$x_best) #> [1] 0.3245835 0.2798031

title: Worked Example: msPCA on mtcars
text: first two dense principal components explain variance, variables receive non-zero loadings. Sparse PCA trades small reduction explained variance much interpretable loading pattern.
code: pca_res <- prcomp(mtcars, scale. = TRUE) fraction_variance_explained(Sigma, pca_res$rotation[, 1:2]) #> [1] 0.8417153

what: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: Sparse PCA multiple principal components R. msPCA package computes sparse loading vectors explain high fraction variance controlling non-redundancy across components. supports two non-redundancy definitions: orthogonality loading vectors, zero pairwise correlation components.

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: Install CRAN: Install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("msPCA") library(msPCA) install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("jeanpauphilet/msPCA") library(msPCA)

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: main function mspca(). Inputs (following elasticnet convention, data single argument M plus type selector): M: data matrix, type: "Sigma" (default) treats M covariance/correlation matrix (p x p); "X" treats M raw data matrix (n observations x p variables), r: number sparse principal components, ks: integer vector length r sparsity budgets. type = "X", mspca() applies algorithm data directly via products t(X) %*% (X %*% beta) never forms p x p matrix. substantially faster memory-efficient n << p. Pass type = "X" whenever number variables greatly exceeds number observations. Output fields: x_best: sparse loading matrix (p x r), objective_value, feasibility_violation, runtime. Example mtcars: Equivalent workflow raw data matrix (covariance matrix needed): datasets n << p, raw-data path avoids O(np^2) cost forming Sigma reduces iteration’s matrix–vector product O(p^2) O(np). Optional dense PCA comparison: Interpretation: Dense PCA usually explains variance. Sparse PCA improves interpretability restricting component small set features. See vignette("msPCA") worked example built mtcars workflow.
code: library(msPCA)  Sigma <- cor(datasets::mtcars) set.seed(42)  res <- mspca(Sigma, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), verbose = FALSE)   # type = "Sigma" is the default print_mspca(res, Sigma)  feasibility_violation_off(Sigma, res$x_best, feasibilityConstraintType = 0) fraction_variance_explained(Sigma, res$x_best) library(msPCA)  X <- as.matrix(datasets::mtcars) set.seed(42)  # type = "X" treats the first argument as raw data; scale = TRUE operates on the # correlation matrix, matching cor(mtcars) above. res <- mspca(X, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), type = "X", scale = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) print_mspca(res)                      # type = "X" results carry their own variance summary  fraction_variance_explained(cor(X), res$x_best) pca_res <- prcomp(datasets::mtcars, scale. = TRUE) fraction_variance_explained(Sigma, pca_res$rotation[, 1:2])

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: ks main tuning input. practical workflow run mspca() multiple sparsity budgets evaluate: fraction variance explained (fraction_variance_explained()), feasibility violation (feasibility_violation_off()), interpretability nonzero loadings.

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: 0 (default): orthogonality constraints loading vectors. 1: zero pairwise correlation constraints components. Use 0 loadings used geometric projection basis. Use 1 statistical decorrelation component scores priority.

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: mspca(M, r, ks, type = c("Sigma", "X"), ...): multiple sparse PCs. tpm(M, k, type = c("Sigma", "X"), ...): single sparse PC via truncated power method. Useful optional arguments mspca(): feasibilityConstraintType feasibilityTolerance maxIter stallingTolerance timeLimitTPM maxRestartTPM minRestartTPM Raw-data arguments (type = "X"): center (default TRUE), scale (default TRUE, set FALSE covariance), divisor ("n-1" sample covariance, default, "n"). Covariance-matrix validation arguments (type = "Sigma"): checkPSD (default TRUE), symTolerance, psdTolerance.

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: fraction_variance_explained(Sigma, U) fraction_variance_explained_perPC(Sigma, U) variance_explained_perPC(Sigma, U) feasibility_violation_off(Sigma, U, feasibilityConstraintType) print_mspca(sol_object, Sigma, digits = 3)

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: Package structure overview: main.R: user-facing functions helper diagnostics. RcppExports.R: R interface compiled code (typically generated Rcpp::compileAttributes()). msPCA_R_CPP.cpp: C++ implementation core algorithm dense/raw-data entry points. CovOperator.h: covariance-operator abstraction (DenseOp Sigma, GramOp X). ConstantArguments.h: internal algorithm constants. RcppExports.cpp: generated C++ interface. Makevars, Makevars.win: compilation settings. man/: function documentation generated roxygen comments. notebook_mtcars.R notebook_plot.R notebook_synthetic.R msPCA_synthetic_results.csv interface changes, regenerate exports documentation Rcpp::compileAttributes() devtools::document().

title: Sparse Principal Component Analysis with Multiple Principal Components
text: See LICENSE.

title: Feasibility Violation — feasibility_violation_off
text: Computes feasibility violation defined \(\sum_{t > s} |u_{t}^\top u_{s}|\) orthogonality constraints enforced (feasibilityConstraintType = 0) \(\sum_{t > s} |u_{t}^\top C u_{s}|\) zero-correlation constraints enforced (feasibilityConstraintType = 1).

title: Feasibility Violation — feasibility_violation_off
code: feasibility_violation_off(C, U, feasibilityConstraintType)

title: Feasibility Violation — feasibility_violation_off
text: C matrix. correlation covariance matrix (p x p). U matrix. column corresponds p-dimensional PC. feasibilityConstraintType integer. Type feasibility constraints enforced. 0: orthogonality constraints; 1: uncorrelatedness constraints.

title: Feasibility Violation — feasibility_violation_off
text: float.

title: Feasibility Violation — feasibility_violation_off
code: TestMat <- cor(mtcars) mspcares <- mspca(TestMat, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), verbose = FALSE) feasibility_violation_off(TestMat, mspcares$x_best, 0) #> [1] 0

title: Multiple Sparse PCA — mspca
text: Returns multiple sparse principal components dataset using iterative deflation heuristic. elasticnet package, data passed single argument M whose interpretation set type: "Sigma" (default) treats M covariance/correlation matrix (p x p) "X" treats M raw data matrix (n observations x p variables). type = "X" algorithm operates data directly via products \(X^\top(X\beta)\) never forms p x p matrix, substantially scalable \(n \ll p\).

title: Multiple Sparse PCA — mspca
code: mspca(   M,   r,   ks,   type = c("Sigma", "X"),   feasibilityConstraintType = 0,   verbose = TRUE,   maxIter = 200,   feasibilityTolerance = 1e-04,   stallingTolerance = 1e-08,   timeLimitTPM = 20,   maxRestartTPM = 30,   minRestartTPM = 20,   center = TRUE,   scale = TRUE,   divisor = c("n-1", "n"),   checkPSD = TRUE,   symTolerance = 1e-08,   psdTolerance = 1e-08 )

title: Multiple Sparse PCA — mspca
text: M matrix. data, interpreted according type: covariance/ correlation matrix (p x p) type = "Sigma", raw data matrix (n x p) type = "X". r integer. Number principal components (PCs) computed. ks integer vector. Target sparsity PC. type (optional) Either "Sigma" (default; M covariance/correlation matrix) "X" (M raw data matrix). feasibilityConstraintType (optional) integer. Type feasibility constraints enforced. 0: orthogonality constraints; 1: uncorrelatedness constraints. Default 0. verbose (optional) Boolean. Controls console output. Default TRUE. maxIter (optional) integer. Maximum number iterations algorithm. Default 200. feasibilityTolerance (optional) float. Tolerance constraint violation (orthogonality/uncorrelatedness, according feasibilityConstraintType). Default 1e-4 stallingTolerance (optional) float. Controls objective improvement algorithm considered stalled. Default 1e-8 timeLimitTPM (optional) integer. Maximum time seconds truncated power method (inner iteration). Default 20. maxRestartTPM (optional) integer. Number random restarts truncated power method (inner iteration) first outer iteration. Default 30. minRestartTPM (optional) integer. Number random restarts truncated power method (inner iteration) outer iterations >= 2. Default 20. center (optional, type = "X") Boolean. Center columns M computing covariance. Default TRUE. scale (optional, type = "X") Boolean. Scale columns M unit variance, .e. operate correlation matrix. Default TRUE. divisor (optional, type = "X") Either "n-1" (default, sample covariance, matches cov/cor) "n" (population covariance). Default "n-1". checkPSD (optional, type = "Sigma") Boolean. Verify M positive semidefinite. Default TRUE. symTolerance (optional, type = "Sigma") float. Tolerance symmetry  check M. Default 1e-8. psdTolerance (optional, type = "Sigma") float. Tolerance (smallest eigenvalue) PSD check M. Default 1e-8.

title: Multiple Sparse PCA — mspca
text: object class "mspca" (list) fields: x_best (p x r matrix sparse PC loadings), objective_value, feasibility_violation, runtime, variance_explained (per-PC explained variance), total_variance (trace covariance matrix). type = "X" additionally records inputType, center, scale, divisor, nObs, p. Use print() display sparse loadings summary() full per-PC breakdown.

title: Multiple Sparse PCA — mspca
code: # From a covariance/correlation matrix (the default type): TestMat <- cor(mtcars) res <- mspca(TestMat, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), verbose = FALSE) print(res, TestMat) #>  #> msPCA solution: 2 sparse PCs #> Pct. variance explained: 32.45835 27.98031  #> Non-zero loadings per PC: 4 4  #>  #> Sparse PCs #>            [,1]       [,2] #> mpg   0.4994876  0.0000000 #> cyl  -0.4952711  0.0000000 #> disp -0.5096594  0.0000000 #> hp    0.0000000  0.5180512 #> wt   -0.4954453  0.0000000 #> qsec  0.0000000 -0.5056634 #> vs    0.0000000 -0.4935970 #> carb  0.0000000  0.4819641 # Equivalent call from the raw data matrix (C need not be passed to print): res_X <- mspca(as.matrix(mtcars), r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), type = "X", verbose = FALSE) print(res_X) #>  #> msPCA solution: 2 sparse PCs #> Pct. variance explained: 32.15198 27.98031  #> Non-zero loadings per PC: 4 4  #>  #> Sparse PCs #>            [,1]       [,2] #> mpg  -0.5250073  0.0000000 #> cyl   0.4063729  0.0000000 #> disp  0.5345591  0.0000000 #> hp    0.0000000 -0.5180502 #> wt    0.5229483  0.0000000 #> qsec  0.0000000  0.5056644 #> vs    0.0000000  0.4935988 #> carb  0.0000000 -0.4819624

title: Summarize an mspca Object — summary.mspca
text: S3 summary method objects class "mspca" returned mspca(). Returns (prints) per-PC summary table (number non-zero loadings, variance explained, FVE, cumulative FVE) together pairwise feasibility violation matrix total solver runtime.

title: Summarize an mspca Object — summary.mspca
code: # S3 method for class 'mspca' summary(object, C = NULL, feasibilityConstraintType = 0L, digits = NULL, ...)

title: Summarize an mspca Object — summary.mspca
text: object object class "mspca", returned mspca(). C (optional) numeric matrix (p x p). covariance correlation matrix used fitting. May omitted type = "X" results, figures stored inside object. feasibilityConstraintType integer. Type constraint used compute feasibility violation reported summary. 0 (default) orthogonality; 1 zero pairwise correlation. digits integer NULL. Number significant digits display. NULL (default), getOption("digits") used. ... arguments passed methods (currently ignored).

title: Summarize an mspca Object — summary.mspca
text: Invisibly returns list class "summary.mspca" fields: table Data frame columns PC, nonzero, variance, fve, cumulative_fve. feasibility_mat r x r matrix pairwise feasibility violations (\(|u_i^\top u_j|\) \(|u_i^\top C u_j|\)). Diagonal lower triangle NA. feasibility Scalar total feasibility violation (sum upper triangle feasibility_mat). runtime Solver runtime seconds (stored object). r Number sparse PCs. inputType "Sigma" "X".

title: Summarize an mspca Object — summary.mspca
code: TestMat <- cor(mtcars) res <- mspca(TestMat, r = 2, ks = c(4, 4), verbose = FALSE) summary(res, TestMat) #>  #> msPCA summary: 2 sparse PC(s) #> Input type   : Sigma  #> Runtime (s)  : 0.012  #>  #> Per-component statistics: #>   PC nonzero variance       fve cumulative_fve #>  PC1       4 3.547820 0.3225291      0.3225291 #>  PC2       4 3.077834 0.2798031      0.6023322 #>  #> Pairwise feasibility violations (upper triangle): #>     PC1 PC2 #> PC1   .   0 #> PC2   .   . #> Total: 0e+00

title: Truncated Power Method — tpm
text: Returns leading sparse principal component dataset using truncated power method. mspca(), data passed single argument M whose interpretation set type: "Sigma" (default) covariance/correlation matrix (p x p) "X" raw data matrix (n x p). See mspca() raw-data preprocessing controls.

title: Truncated Power Method — tpm
code: tpm(   M,   k,   type = c("Sigma", "X"),   maxIter = 200,   verbose = TRUE,   timeLimit = 10,   center = TRUE,   scale = FALSE,   divisor = c("n-1", "n"),   checkPSD = TRUE,   symTolerance = 1e-08,   psdTolerance = 1e-08 )

title: Truncated Power Method — tpm
text: M matrix. data, interpreted according type: covariance/ correlation matrix (p x p) type = "Sigma", raw data matrix (n x p) type = "X". k integer. Target sparsity PC. type (optional) Either "Sigma" (default; M covariance/correlation matrix) "X" (M raw data matrix). maxIter (optional) integer. Maximum number iterations algorithm. Default 200. verbose (optional) Boolean. Controls console output. Default TRUE. timeLimit (optional) integer. Maximum time seconds. Default 10. center (optional, type = "X") Boolean. Center columns M. Default TRUE. scale (optional, type = "X") Boolean. Scale columns M unit variance. Default FALSE. divisor (optional, type = "X") Either "n-1" (default) "n". checkPSD (optional, type = "Sigma") Boolean. Verify M PSD. Default TRUE. symTolerance (optional, type = "Sigma") float. Symmetry-check tolerance. Default 1e-8. psdTolerance (optional, type = "Sigma") float. PSD-check tolerance. Default 1e-8.

title: Truncated Power Method — tpm
text: object class "tpm" (list) fields: x_best (p x 1 matrix containing sparse PC loading), objective_value, runtime. type = "X" additionally records inputType, center, scale, divisor, nObs, p.

title: Truncated Power Method — tpm
text: Yuan, X. T., & Zhang, T. (2013). Truncated power method sparse eigenvalue problems. Journal Machine Learning Research, 14(1), 899–925.

title: Variance Explained Per PC — variance_explained_perPC
text: Computes variance explained PC.

title: Variance Explained Per PC — variance_explained_perPC
code: variance_explained_perPC(C, U)

title: Variance Explained Per PC — variance_explained_perPC
text: C matrix. correlation covariance matrix (p x p). U matrix. matrix containing r PCs (p x r).

title: Variance Explained Per PC — variance_explained_perPC
text: numeric vector length ncol(U).

title: msPCA 0.5.0
text: mspca() tpm() now take two possible inputs: covariance/correlation matrix data matrix directly. practice, functions take single generic argument M together type = c("Sigma", "X") selector. type = "Sigma" (default) treats M covariance/correlation matrix (p x p); type = "X" treats M raw data matrix (n observations x p variables). "Sigma" default preserves behaviour existing matrix-based calls. raw-data path applies algorithm data directly: product Sigma %*% beta evaluated t(X) %*% (X %*% beta) / (n - 1) cost O(np), p x p matrix never materialized. substantially improves scalability n << p. covariance back-end refactored behind covariance-operator abstraction (DenseOp / GramOp) shared input modes. Added preprocessing controls type = "X": center, scale (covariance vs correlation), divisor (“n-1” “n”). Added validation input modes: Sigma input checked squareness, symmetry positive semidefiniteness (checkPSD, symTolerance, psdTolerance); X input checked finiteness, dimensions (scaling) zero-variance columns. mspca() results now include variance_explained (per-PC) total_variance; X-mode results also record inputType, center, scale, divisor, nObs p. mspca() tpm() now return S3 objects class "mspca" "tpm" respectively, enabling use standard R generics. Created methods print.mspca() (replacing print_mspca(), now deprecated), summary.mspca().

title: msPCA 0.4.1
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-12 Standardized function man page titles consistent title style. Removed unnecessary library(datasets) calls examples keeping explicit datasets::mtcars usage, added datasets Suggests align example dependencies CRAN guidance. Improved efficiency clarity R code Added vignette

title: msPCA 0.4.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-22 Renamed hyperparameters controlling truncated power method restart budgets clearer consistent API naming. Documentation polish across function docs package materials. Removed pairwise_correlation() orthogonality_violation() replaced unified feasibility_violation_off() helper feasibility diagnostics across constraint types.

title: msPCA 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-15 Improved scalability mspca() tpw() algorithmic implementation optimizations. Function mspca() now accepts new hyper-parameter minRestartTPM limits number random restarts truncated power method first outer iteration Improved scaling penalty parameters case zero-correlation constraints Fixed incorrect acronym truncated power method (TPW <- TPM)

title: msPCA 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-12 Added support -correlation constraints PCs well orthogonality constraints. User chooses orthogonality uncorrelatedness constraints via feasibilityConstraintType parameter msPCA(). Renamed return field orthogonality_violation feasibility_violation support constraint types. Renamed function feasibility_violation() orthogonality_violation() explicit Created function pairwise_correlation() Added warning message feasible solution found

title: msPCA 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-12-09 Initial CRAN release
